perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” means any da ta or information that is proprietary to the Disclosing Pa rty and not generally known to the public, whether in tangible or intangible form, whene ver and however disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, operations, sales estimates, business plans and performance results relating to the past, present or future business activities of su ch party, its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (ii) plans for products or services, and customer or supp lier lists; (iii) any s cientific or technical information, invention, de sign, process, procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; (iv) any con cepts, reports, data, know-how, works-in-progress, designs, development tools, specifications, co mputer software, so urce code, object code, flow charts, databases, inventions, information and trade secrets; and (v) any o ther information that should reasonably be recognized as confidential information of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” means data or information that the Disclosing Party deems proprietary, whether or not it is generally known to the public. This includes, without limitation: (i) marketing strategies, plans, financial data, projections, operations, sales estimates, business plans, and performance results relating to the past, present, or future business activities of the party, its affiliates, subsidiaries, and affiliated companies; (ii) product or service plans, and customer or supplier lists; (iii) scientific or technical data, inventions, designs, processes, procedures, formulas, improvements, technologies, or methods; (iv) concepts, reports, data, know-how, works-in-progress, designs, development tools, specifications, computer software, source code, object code, flow charts, databases, inventions, information, and trade secrets; and (v) any other information the Disclosing Party designates as confidential.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of "Confidential Information" has been altered to allow the Disclosing Party to define information as confidential regardless of its public availability. This undermines the standard legal definition of confidential information, which typically excludes information already known to the public.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition allows the Disclosing Party to designate publicly known information as "Confidential Information". The Economic Espionage Act (EEA) defines "trade secret" (a subset of Confidential Information) in a way that requires the information to not be generally known or readily ascertainable. By including publicly known information, the contract's definition contradicts the EEA's definition of a trade secret.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modified contract redefines "Confidential Information" to include data the Disclosing Party deems proprietary, irrespective of public knowledge. This directly opposes the standard legal definition of trade secrets, a subset of confidential information, which, as suggested by the Law Explanation and supported by the Economic Espionage Act (EEA), requires that the information not be generally known. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 only points to the Economic Espionage Act and doesn't define trade secrets directly, Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 18 U.S.C. 1839, implies definitions are associated with the protection of trade secrets, therefore redefining confidential information in this manner contradicts the intent and potential application of trade secret laws. The altered definition permits designating publicly available information as confidential, undermining the legal basis for protecting genuine trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information need not be novel, unique, patentable, copyrightable or constitute a trade secret in order to be designated Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information must not be novel, unique, patentable, copyrightable or constitute a trade secret in order to be designated Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'need not' to 'must not', the contract now requires that confidential information *cannot* be a trade secret, which is nonsensical and contradicts common legal understanding and potentially invalidates the purpose of the NDA.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines trade secrets, and this modified clause excludes them from being considered confidential information under the contract. This contradicts the core purpose of most NDAs, which is to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: trade secret | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you trade secret The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) is a piece of legislation created by the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) . The UTSA defines trade secrets and describes claims related to trade secrets. As of 2024, 48 states , the District of Columbia, the U.S. Virgin Islands, and Puerto Rico, have adopted the UTSA. What is a Trade Secret UTSA's Definition The UTSA defines a " trade secret " as: Information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. Non-UTSA Definition Prior to the development of the UTSA, improper use or disclosure of a trade secret was traditionally a common law tort . Sections 757 and 758 of the Restatement of Torts (1939) set forth the basic principles of trade secret law that were widely adopted by U.S. courts . In particular, §757, comment b, listed six factors to be considered in determining whether information constitutes a trade secret: The extent to which the information is known outside the claimant's business. The extent to which it is known by employees and others involved in the business. The extent of measures taken by the claimant to guard the secrecy of the information. The value of the information to the business and its competitors. The amount of effort or money expended by the business in developing the information. The ease or difficulty with which the information could be properly acquired or duplicated by others. Elements of a Trade Secret Claim There are typically three essential elements to a trade secret claim: The subject matter involved must qualify for trade secret protection (see "Scope" below for more on this). The holder of the subject matter must establish that reasonable precautions were taken to prevent disclosure of the subject matter. The trade secret holder must prove that the information was misappropriated or wrongfully taken. Use of a trade secret belonging to another does not always constitute misappropriation. There are two basic situations in which obtaining the use of a trade secret is illegal; where it is acquired through improper means, or where it involves a breach of confidence. Trade secrets may be obtained by lawful means such as independent discovery, reverse engineering, and inadvertent disclosure resulting from the trade secret holder's failure to take reasonable protective measures. The misappropriation of trade secrets is considered a form of unfair competition , and is discussed in the Restatement (Third) of Unfair Competition . Misappropriation of Trade Secrets as a Crime In some circumstances, misappropriation of trade secrets is not only a tort ; it is a federal crime . Economic Espionage Act In 1996, Congress enacted the Economic Espionage Act ("EEA"), codified as 18 U.S.C. §§1831-1839 . In United States v. Sazonov , the defendant Sazonov has been charged with violating the EEA for "attempt[ing] to steal valuable proprietary computer code that took his employer years to develop . . . Sazonov was employed for thirteen years by Susquehanna International Group ("Susquehanna"), a financial services firm headquartered in Pennsylvania with offices in Manhattan." Scope of Subject Matter Customer Lists Customer lists and other lists related to customer business qualify for trade secret protection if the lists' information cannot be ascertained from other generally available sources. In Morlife Inc. v. Perry, 56 Cal. App. 4th 1514 (1997) , the California Court held that customer identities from an organization's list are protected as trade secrets if the identities are not generally kno
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction. The original text states that Confidential Information 'need not' be a trade secret. The modified text states that Confidential Information 'must not' be a trade secret. This contradicts the definition of a trade secret as described in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which defines a trade secret and describes claims related to trade secrets. The Law Explanation specifies this change excludes trade secrets from being considered confidential information, contradicting the core purpose of most NDAs. Therefore, the change introduces a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding an ything in the foregoing to the contrary, Confidential Information shall not include information which: (i) was known by the Receiving Pa rty prior to receiving the Confidential Information from the Disclosing Party; (b) becomes rightfully known to the Receiving Party from a third-party source not known (after diligent inquiry) by the Receiving Party to be under an obligation to Disclosing Party to maintain confidentiality; (c) is or becomes publicly available through no fault of or failure to act by the Rec eiving Party in breach of this Agreement; (d) is required to be disclosed in a judicial or administrative proceeding, or is otherwise requested or required to be disclosed by law or regulation, although the requirements of paragraph 4 hereof shall apply prior to any disclosure being made; and (e) is or has been independently developed by employees, consultants or agents of the Receiving Party without violation of the terms of this Agreement or reference or access to any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding anything to the contrary, Confidential Information shall not include information which: (i) was known by the Receiving Party prior to receiving the Confidential Information from the Disclosing Party; (b) becomes known to the Receiving Party from any source; (c) is or becomes publicly available, regardless of fault; (d) is required to be disclosed in a judicial or administrative proceeding, without prior notice to the Disclosing Party; and (e) is independently developed by employees of the Receiving Party, regardless of access to Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification broadens the exclusions from the definition of Confidential Information to include information obtained from 'any source,' publicly available information regardless of fault, disclosure without notice, and independently developed information regardless of access. This significantly weakens the protections afforded to the Disclosing Party's confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement of Torts § 757 addresses liability for the disclosure or use of another's trade secret. The modified clause weakens the protection of trade secrets by allowing disclosure of publicly available information and independently developed information, even if derived from the confidential information. This undermines the legal basis for trade secret protection as articulated in the Restatement.

The UTSA defines trade secrets and describes claims related to trade secrets. As of 2024, 48 states , the District of Columbia, the U.S. Virgin Islands, and Puerto Rico, have adopted the UTSA. What is a Trade Secret UTSA's Definition The UTSA defines a " trade secret " as: Information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. Sections 757 and 758 of the Restatement of Torts (1939) set forth the basic principles of trade secret law that were widely adopted by U.S. courts . In particular, §757, comment b, listed six factors to be considered in determining whether information constitutes a trade secret: The extent to which the information is known outside the claimant's business. The extent to which it is known by employees and others involved in the business. The extent of measures taken by the claimant to guard the secrecy of the information. The ease or difficulty with which the information could be properly acquired or duplicated by others. perturbation.justification: The modification significantly weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially undermining trade secret law. The original text provided specific exceptions to what constitutes confidential information, maintaining some level of protection for the Disclosing Party. The changed text broadens these exceptions, such as including information from 'any source' and removing fault considerations for publicly available information. This directly contradicts the principle that reasonable efforts must be made to maintain secrecy. The first snippet states that a trade secret derives value from 'not being generally known' and requires 'reasonable' efforts to maintain secrecy. By allowing disclosure of information regardless of its source or the fault of the Receiving Party, the modified contract undermines the Disclosing Party's ability to maintain the secrecy required for trade secret protection. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Each party warrants that it has the right to make the disclosures under this Agreement. NO WARRANT IES ARE MADE BY EITHER PART Y UNDE R THIS AGREE MENT WHATSOE VER . The parties acknowledge that although they shall each endeavor to include in the Confidential Information all information that they each believe relevant for the purpose of the evaluation of a Tran saction, the parties understand that no representation or warranty as to the accuracy or co mpleteness of the Confidential Information is being made by either party as the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party warrants that it has the right to make the disclosures under this Agreement.  Each party warrants the completeness and accuracy of the provided confidential information. The parties acknowledge that they shall include in the Confidential Information all information that they each believe relevant for the purpose of the evaluation of a Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: This modification adds a warranty of completeness and accuracy. Warranties can create legal obligations and liabilities. While seemingly beneficial, if the Disclosing Party's information contains unintentional errors or omissions, this warranty creates a basis for a breach of contract claim. It increases the risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: The addition of a warranty regarding the accuracy of the confidential information could lead to legal complications under Newfoundland's Misrepresentation Act. If the Disclosing Party makes a false statement of fact (even innocently) that induces the Receiving Party to enter into the agreement, the Receiving Party may have grounds to rescind the contract or claim damages. The original contract explicitly disclaims any warranty as to accuracy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: House of Asembly - NL - Error Page It seems JavaScript is either disabled or not supported by your browser. Please enable JavaScript to improve your experience. House of Assembly Newfoundland and Labrador Sorry, this page could not be found! The House of Assembly website has recently been updated. Please visit our new homepage .
perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly disclaims any warranty regarding the accuracy of confidential information, whereas the changed text introduces a warranty of completeness and accuracy. This directly contradicts the original agreement by creating a new legal obligation. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is an error page, the LLM explanation points to the Misrepresentation Act, suggesting the warranty creates a new basis for legal action. This is a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction, as the original language and the changed language conflict, making the contract unenforceable as written due to conflicting terms.

perturbation.original_text: The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Newfoundland, Canada applicable to contracts made and to be wholly performed within such state, without giving effect to any conflict of laws provisions thereof. The Federal and state courts located in Newfoundland, Canada , shall have sole and exc lusive jurisdiction over any disputes arising under the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Ontario, Canada, regardless of where the contract is performed. The courts of Newfoundland, Canada, shall have non-exclusive jurisdiction over any disputes arising under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Ontario while maintaining jurisdiction in Newfoundland creates a conflict. Furthermore, changing the jurisdiction to 'non-exclusive' allows disputes to be heard in other jurisdictions, potentially undermining the intended forum.
perturbation.law_explanation: Canadian conflict of law rules generally respect the parties' choice of governing law. However, the change to Ontario law combined with Newfoundland jurisdiction creates uncertainty and could invite challenges to the enforceability of the forum selection clause. Canadian courts may refuse to enforce a forum selection clause if it is unfair or unreasonable. The conflict introduced makes the clause more vulnerable.

Legislative history: 2023, c. 2, Sched. 4, s. 2 . Lien on goods for accommodation, etc. 2 (1) An innkeeper has a lien on the goods of a guest for the value or price of any food or accommodation furnished to the guest or on the guestâs account.Â R.S.O. 1990, c.Â I.7, s.Â 2Â (1). Power to sell (2) In addition to all other remedies provided by law, an innkeeper has the right, in case the same remains unpaid for three months, to sell by public auction the goods of the guest, on giving one weekâs notice of the intended sale by advertisement in a newspaper published in the municipality in which the inn is situate or, in case there is no newspaper published in the municipality, in a newspaper published nearest to the inn.Â R.S.O. 1990, c.Â I.7, s.Â 2Â (2). Particulars in notice (3) The advertisement shall state the name of the guest, the amount of indebtedness, the time and place of sale, and the name of the auctioneer, and shall give a description of the goods to be sold.Â R.S.O. 1990, c.Â I.7, s.Â 2Â (3). Lien on horses and carriages 3 (1) The keeper of a livery stable or a boarding stable has a lien on every horse or other animal boarded at or carriage left in the livery stable or boarding stable for reasonable charges for boarding and caring for the horse, animal or carriage.Â R.S.O. 1990, c.Â I.7, s.Â 3Â (1). Lien on horses, etc., and power to sell (2) An innkeeper, livery-stable keeper or boarding-stable keeper who has a lien upon a horse, other animal or carriage for the value or price of any food or accommodation supplied, or for care or labour bestowed thereon, has, in addition to all other remedies provided by law, the right, in case the same remains unpaid for two weeks, to sell by public auction the horse, animal or carriage on giving two weeks notice of the intended sale by advertisement in a newspaper published in the municipality in which the inn, livery stable or boarding stable is situate or, in case there is no newspaper published in the municipality, in a newspaper published nearest to the inn, livery stable or boarding stable.Â R.S.O. 1990, c.Â I.7, s.Â 3Â (2). Advertisement of intended sale (3) The advertisement shall state the name, if known, of the person or persons who brought the horse, animal or carriage to the inn, livery stable or boarding stable, the amount of the indebtedness, and the name of the auctioneer, and shall give a description of the horse, animal or carriage.Â R.S.O. 1990, c.Â I.7, s.Â 3Â (3). Proceeds of sale, application (4) The innkeeper, livery-stable keeper or boarding-stable keeper may apply the proceeds of the sale in payment of the amount due, and the costs of the advertisement and sale, and shall pay over the surplus, if any, to the person entitled thereto on application being made therefor.Â R.S.O. 1990, c.Â I.7, s.Â 3Â (4). Non-application â Ontario Feeder Cattle Loan Guarantee Program (5) This section does not apply with respect to cattle enrolled in the Ontario Feeder Cattle Loan Guarantee Program established under the Ministry of Agriculture, Food and Rural Affairs Act . 2023, c. 2,
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction. The original text specifies that the laws of Newfoundland, Canada, govern the contract, and any disputes will be under the sole and exclusive jurisdiction of courts in Newfoundland. The changed text alters the governing law to Ontario, Canada, but maintains jurisdiction in Newfoundland, while also changing the jurisdiction to 'non-exclusive'. This misalignment, as pointed out in the Perturbation Explanation, introduces uncertainty and potential challenges to the enforceability of the forum selection clause. The Law Explanation also suggests that such a conflict could make the clause more vulnerable. While the provided scraped snippets do not directly address conflict of law principles, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation sufficiently indicate a legal contradiction exists due to the conflicting governing law and jurisdiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any failure by either party to enforce the other party’s strict performance of any provision of this Agreement will not constitute a waiver of its right to subsequently enforce such provision or any other provision of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Any failure by either party to enforce the other party’s strict performance of any provision of this Agreement will constitute a waiver of its right to subsequently enforce that specific provision.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'or any other provision of this Agreement', the clause implies that failure to enforce one provision waives only that provision, not the whole agreement. While subtle, this changes the legal effect of non-enforcement and could lead to disputes about the extent of the waiver.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under common law principles of waiver, a party's conduct can sometimes be interpreted as a waiver of rights, even if that wasn't the party's explicit intention. The original clause explicitly prevents a single instance of non-enforcement from being interpreted as a broader waiver. The modified clause makes it easier to argue that a waiver occurred, potentially contradicting the parties' initial intent. It also ignores the precedent in law where 'waiver' may not be limited to that specific provision.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: waiver | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you waiver A waiver is the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege . In legal contexts, a waiver is often documented in writing, but it can also be implied through actions . By waiving a right, an individual or party chooses not to enforce a particular right, claim , or provision they are legally entitled to. [Last reviewed in July of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex accidents and injuries COMMERCE commercial activities LIFE EVENTS accidents & injuries (tort law) THE LEGAL PROCESS courts criminal procedure business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure individual rights legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text alters the effect of a waiver, potentially contradicting legal precedent and the parties' initial intent. The original text prevents a single instance of non-enforcement from being interpreted as a broader waiver, while the changed text makes it easier to argue that a waiver occurred. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines a waiver as 'the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege' and also states it can be 'implied through actions.' The modified clause weakens the protection against implied waivers, making it easier to argue that a waiver has occurred even if unintended, thus creating a legal contradiction. By limiting the scope of the non-waiver provision, it increases the risk that conduct will be construed as an implied waiver, going against the purpose of the original clause.
